CURRENT AFFAIRS.

L‘udenbly in the matter of wire mattresses it is “‘ No
Irish Need Apply’’ so far as the patriotic Board of
- Guardians of Newcastle West (Limerick) Union are con-
cerned. . We take the following advt. from a local
paper:—-

NEWCASTLE WEST UNION.
~ SPRING MATTRESSES WANTED.

The Boa.rd of Guardians will, on Thursday, 31st Oct.,
1907, receive tenders for the supplymg of 2 dozen of
Te¥’ ‘Domplex Chippold Spring Mattresses, 6 feet long
3 feet: v(,\de, and accordmg to sample to be seen at
orkhouse.

According to our information there are four wire
mattress makers in Dublin and three in Belfast. One
of the Dublin makers supplied goods to the Viceregal
Lodge during Lord Dudley’s time, but what was good
enough for the Viceregal Lodge—though made in Ire-
land—may not be good enough in the estimation of the
patriotic Board of Guardians of Newcastle West for the
paupers! And so, no Irish manufacturer need apply,
for the wire maftresses for the Union must be made in
Birmingham as per advertisement! Might we suggest
that the patriotic Guardians of Newcastle West shonld
pass a pulverising resolution against Lord Dudley, ex-
Lord Lieutenant, for insulting the Viceregal Lodge by
dumping an Irish-made wire mattress into it—an insult
that the Guardians have taken care shall not be re-
peated so far as the Newcastle West poorhouse is con-
cerned. God save Ireland!

A South African reader sends us a cutting from
“The Friend,”” Bloemfontein, of September 30th.
The headings to the cutting are—‘ Colonial Industries.
A Laudable Movement. Mafeking Ladies Meet.
Support for Colonial Articles.””  There was a public
meeting in the Mafeking Town Hall under the presi-
dency of the Mayoress, Mrs. Joyce, to urge the support
of colonial industries by organised effort. ~ We read
that the idea was enthusiastically adopted, and that
nearly one hundred ladies decided to band themselves
togéther, and the following pledge was signed before
the meeting dispersed :—‘‘ We, the undersigned ladies
of Mafeking, bind ourselves to do all in our power to
encourage South African industry in every branch,
household, personal, agricultural and manufacturing.
With that object in view we further bind ourselves
that we will, wherever possible, satisfy our require-
ments by purchasing locally-produced articles in pre-
ference to imported articles. . Lastly, we undertake fo
do all in our power to combat the still existing preju-
dice against colonial products, and inculcate the doc-
trine of encouragement of colonial industry as an essen-
tial part of South African patriotism.”” This is a new
sort of ‘‘ Mafeking *’ that we can heartily recommend
to our lady readers in all parts of Ireland and outside
Ireland.

A correspondent sends us a post-card published by
Lawrence, of Dublin, and asks us—‘‘ Can nothing be
done to these ‘ Sackville’ Street atrocities?’ The only
ways that we can think of helping to put a stop to
them is—(1) refraining from buying them, and (2) by
customers of the shops in which they are offered for
sale protesting against their sale. The particular post-
card represents a drunken ‘‘Paddy’’ catching some
railings, and replying to a policeman’s request to move
on. “ Arrah, lave me alone, sure it’s playin’ the harp
I am.”  We have referred to this thing for amusing.
fools before—indeed, we think we referred to it many
times. We don’t suppose that any of our readers would -
buy such a thing, but we cannot answer for the
benighted readers of the ‘ Freeman’’ and the “ Inde-
pondent.’” -

During the month of October a temperance crusade
under the auspices of the Anti-Treating League is
being carried on in the County Wexford. Besides the
priests of the House of Missions, Enniscorthy, -there are
ten Redemptorists engaged in the crusade for the whole
month of October, and twelve for part of the time.
They preach the crusade over thirty parishes, and in
more than twice that' number of churches. So far, we:
lear, the crusade has been a decided success. One of
the ob]ects of the crusade is to combat, and if possible |
suppress, drinking at threshing and hay—makmg A
grand temperance revival throughout the diocese is the

general result hoped for and aimed at. As a rule, the
priests devote one week to each parish. ~Where the
parish is divided between two churches, which is
generally the case, the priests preach on the first four
nights—from Sunday to Wednesday included—in one;
and the four last nights in the other—Wednesday to
Sunday. On Wednesday night they proach in both,
When passing from one parish to another at the end
of the week, if the parishes are near, they will generally
be able to give a sermon in both parishes on the Sun-
day also, concluding in one, and opening in the other.
Where there are more, or less, than two churches
specm[ arrangements are made. The drinking abt
tln:cshmgs has often been referred to in our columns
It is a custom not unknown even in the County of Wex-
ford.  Although not equally bad in all districts, the
custom of supplying large quantities of intoxicating
liquor at threshings is, we hear, now generally recog-
unised as one of the worst drinking customs in the
diocese. _ The quantity of drink consumed has been
steadily increasing year by year. Not only is drinking
universal at threshings and common at “hay-making ;
but of late it has in some places spread even to the har-
vest field.  The Anti-Treating League has done very
considerable work in Ireland, and we are sure that by
the end of October some Wexford contributor will be
in a position to write us a glowing account of the effects
of the temperance mission.

The Irish Veterinary College is supplying some vets.
to the British army, and during the last two years the
Dublin College sent two of its graduates to India, one
to the Malay Federated States and another to Rhodesia.
Can it be that Irish vets. are only valued at a distance,
for in the county of Galway they have a British
vet., and the vet. is a woman! We don’t wish to get
into a controversy with the suffragettes, but certainly
we must say that the office of public veterinary surgeon
in Galway is not one that appears to us to suit one of
““ the gentle sex.”

The veterinary surgeon in question is, we are in-
formed, a relation of Mr. George Wyndham, an ex-Cnief
Secretary. Whether his influence had anything to do
with bringing about the appointment or not we cannot
say. The lady veterinary surgeon of Mount Bellew
Union (Co. Galway) pursued her studies in Edinburgh
with distinction. But the authorities of the Edinburgh
Veterinary College refused to give her the Diploma.
The lady then presented herself for examination in
England, and the English examining body, it would
appear, got over the difficulty by requiring a two years’
practice of the work of the profession from all candi-
dates, and the veterinary surgeon to the Mount Bellew
Union (Co. Galway) apparently could not, certainly
she did not, find any public body either in England or
Scotland to appoint her to a position in which she could
secure this two years’ practice of her profession. But
when all countries fail, welcome green Ireland !

The -position of V.S. to the Mount Bellew Union
became vacant, and the aristocratic lady from England
who was refused a Diploma in Scotland and did not
find scope for practice in England was elected V.S. by
the Galway County Council. This appointment was
subject to ratification by the Department of Agricul-
ture. And, as a matter of fact, the aid of the Depart-
ment was sought with a view to counteracting the work
of the Galway County Council! What a characteristic
picture in this topsy-turvy country of green patriols.
Iere you have the Department appealed to to save the

THE LEADER.

OCTOBER 26, 1907. -

patriotic County Council of Galway frgm appointing
an Bnglish V.S. to Mount Bellew Union—a woman
V.S., too, to whom Edinburgh did not give a Diploma
and to whom England did not give professional work.
The Department, on hechnical‘grou‘nds, r{:[used' o
sanction the appointment of this aristocratic British
woman as V.S. to Mount Bellew Union.

But the patriotic Galway County Council was nob
going to take its repulse lying down. If the Depart-
ment did not sanction its British V.S. it was going to
know the reason why. The Galway County Council
appointed the British V.S. again, and on the second
occasion, as there was no opposition, it appears bl{at the
Department had no option but Lp_conﬁrm her in her
position.  In taking the position she gave an
undertaking that, when she had fulfilled the require-
ments of the English Veterinary Department, namely,
practising her profession for two years, she would
resign and return to England. The two years have now
elapsed, but the British lady veterinary surgeon of
Mount Bellew Union holds on to the post.

Now that Treland has a Veterinary College of its
own that has supplied vets. to India, the British army,
and other places, the Mount Bellew Union might at
least give an Irishman the appointment.  Apart from
the objections to a woman holding such a public position
it is a strange thing to have the Galway County Council
making a fight for an importation even against the
Department.

Mr. Willie Redmond was presented with an address
of welcome at Feakle, in the County of Clare TIn the
address he was greeted with a ‘ cead mille failthe”’ to
Fcakle.  The spelling of the italicised words is the
same as that which appears in the newspaper report
before us. Ten years ago “‘ mille failthe *’ might have
been excused, but really at the present time it is unpar-
donable; Mr. Willie Redmond, who knows some Irish,
must have felt uneasy at such spelling. The bad
spelling is not the only outstanding feature of the
address. If we put the address bodily into such a book
as ‘“Tom O’Kelly,” we would probably be charged
with exaggeration. Here is a paragraph from the
Feakle address:— ‘It is now nigh fifteen years since
the quick-witted men of Clare took you to their bosom.
They saw in you the man of culture, the dignified and
true patriot; they stood by you in a time of trial and
stress, and for a moment they did no% waver till they
elected you their member of Parliament by a sweeping
majority.”’ It is hard to know whether to laugh or
sigh at such stuff. So the ‘‘quick-witted men of
Clare ”’ took Mr. Willie Redmond ‘‘ to their bosom,’”
did they! Oh, Clare, God help us!

The address goes on:— The men who showed such
sterling manhood, grit, and courage then are here
to-day, and, if needs be, are as ready and fit to-day as
any time during those fifteen eventful years to stand
by you to support you.””  The ¢ quick-witted men of
Clare” who took Mr. Willie Redmond ““to their
bosom,” and who showed apparently by doing so
‘“ such sterling manhood, grit and courage,” are ‘ here
to-day.” It was a wonder that they were not ‘ stand-
ing here to-day!'” By the way, what of the Ennis
Bungs? Have they taken Mr. Willie Redmond to their
bosom? 1T they had him in one of the barrels it isn’t
too quick they’d be, maybe, in letbing him out, poor
man! Where is Mr. O’Moloney, of Ennis?

CTOBER 26, 1907.

One of the famous “ Bells of Shandon * is to be re-
placed, and the contract has been placed in—Britain.
The Incumbent of St. Anne’s, Shandon, offers as an ex-
pl{mahon that he invited a tender from a well-known
Irish firm of bell founders in ‘Dublin, but the letter
camo back marked “ Gone away.” It appears that the
fixm in question has been extinct for some yoars, bub
that the business is being carried on in an adjoining
street under a new name and by a new firm. Well, for
our part, though we exceedingly regret that a Shandon
Bell is to be imported, we have no sympathy with the
Dublin bell founder. Why does he not announce him-
self ; one would think that the very nature of the work
—the making of ringing bells— would predispose him
Lo telling the public about himself and his business. e
has lost the contract now. There are some manufac-
turers and if they spent a fow shillings in the insertion
of an advertisement in a paper, and if, as a consequence,
a number of people did not call up in a fow aying
—‘* Having seen your advt. in so and so, I have come
to order a peal of bells, or an organ, or a whole church,’’
as the case might be, they would cry over their few
shillings and think that they had thrown it away. If
there are more bell founders than one in Ireland they
had better hurry up and announce their existence in our
columns before the next order for bells goes out of the
country.

Dirty Dublin had a treat on Monday night, and
Dirty Dublin rose to the occasion. We read in Tues-
day’s ‘“Irish Times’’:—“. . . but we confess
that we were unprepared for the enormous crowd that
blocked every entrance to the Gaiety last night. So
huge was the crowd and so long the line of carriages
outside the theatre that the house might almost have
been filled twice over, while the last person had not been
able to reach his seat until the play was fairly well
advanced.” The Pastoral Kopje climbing and ‘‘ Truly
Catholic >> “‘ Freeman ’’ gives nearly a column of quite
rapturous notice to this imported thing. No wonder,
in its heart, Irish Ireland distrusts the ‘ Freeman,’”’
and the distrust naturally and deservedly extends to
what the ‘“ Freeman ’’ stands for in politics and matters
concerning nationality. It refers to ‘ Mr. Pinero’s
wonderfully able work.”” It remarks:—‘‘ And so from
the moment the curtain rises to the end, the audience
is captured by the glamour of its wonderfully captivating
contrast of character, and the wit and brilliancy of its
dialogue.”” Let us turn to the ‘“ Independent.”” That
paper gave the piece about one inch longer notice on
its leading page than was occupied away back on page
six by His Grace the Archbishop of Dublin’s letter 1¢
St. Agatha’s Church. Indeed, it is a wonder that both
the ‘“ Freeman ’’ and the ‘‘ Independent ’’ had not long
articles in support of some Magdalen Asylum in Tues-
day’s issue, it would have been a ‘‘ wonderfully capti-
vating contrast of character.”

The ‘“Independent’ said:—‘‘ The Pinero play was

applauded last night through the various stages of its

THE LEADER.

147

progress. The audience seemed to derive pleasure from
having its feelings harrowed, but it’s fair to say also
they very warmly marked their appreciation of .the
manner in which the play was handled.”” So Dirty
Dublin that went in carriages, on trams, and on foot
‘“applauded last night.”” Unfortunately, we are not
surprised.

Now this fetid piece of Mr. Pinero’s was the firsu
imported play that we dealt with in our series on ‘‘ The
Lnglish Mind in Ireland.” We dealt with it in our
second number, September 8th, 1900, over seven years
ago, and here is the filthy thing back again, a magnet
once more for Dirty Dublin. The following is part of
what we said of the thing seven years ago:—‘ The hero
in his early days lived, in the cant phrase of the male
society depicted here on the stage, what is called a
‘man’s life.’”  Most unsophisticated people in thie
country would, of course, prefer to call it a ‘black-
guard’s life ’; and a person leading such a life would
scarcely be on speaking terms with his neighbours. He
marries somebody else afterwards, a Catholic, whose
cold temperament is described by the elegant remark
that she kept a thermometer in her stays which regis-
tered 10 degrees below zero’; and by the further asser-
tions that, ‘she had fever—the only warmth that ever
came to her.” The wife dies, and the play opens when
the husband is about to marry ‘a woman with a past.”
This man’s dearest friend and confidant had already
led ‘a man’s life’ with this very woman. A whole-
some situation! The marriage does not turn out a very
good working arrangement. The new wife is jealous of
her step-daughter, who shrinks instinctively from the
adventuress. The play develops along until the step-
daughter-—an ingenue from a Convent—falls in love
with a dashing officer who does gallant things in India
and wins the Victoria Cross. One is being worked up
to the melting mood over this dainty love story; and
the sentimental girls in the audience are searching for
their handkerchiefs, when the dashing officer is intro-
duced by his adored to her step-mother. The dashing
officer recognises his prospective mother-in-law, for the
fact is that he, too, had at one time lived ‘a man’s
life* with her. Another wholesome situation! In the
development that takes place, the dashing officer pro-
tests to this woman: ‘I always treated you fairly.” For
the typical British mind is fast coming to the conclusion
that Heaven can be bribed and hell cheated with money
down. ¢ After all,” he further says, ‘I have been just
like other fellows.”

“We will not pursue this morbid, fetid plot. A
few of the smart sayings may be given by way of fur-
ther illustration of the.tone of the piece. Servants are
only machines to wait upon you and give evidence in
the Divorce Court.” This is typically British. It is an
attitude of mind that will eventually be the undoing of
the race. For a nation cannot develop indefinitely on
the basis that men are mere penny-in-the-slot. machines

‘Some seven or eight years ago, when the Industrial
Revival was young, those who pinned their faith.in its
development found much to encourage.them in the great:
success of many existing Irish industries—Shipbuilding,
Cloths, Tobaccos, ete., alrcady ecmphatically de-
monstrated the potentiality of TIrish enterprise in pro-
ductive  channels. The well-known ink manu-
factured in  Dublin by Messrs. Bewley and
Draper, Ltd., was amongst those already thriv-
ing Trish industries that made the carly Industrial
Revivalists optimistic. ~ Bewley & Draper have been
manufacturing their ink in Dublin for now close on half
a century, and years ago it had become almost a house-
hold word in this country and won extensive popularity
in various places abroad. - There are two forms of story-
telling obviously hurtful to Irish industry. One is to
pass off a foreign product as Irish-made, and the other
i3 to slander an Irish-made article by suggesting it is
foreign made. Somebody’s invention has been busy re-
cently in the latter class of fiction in connection with
Bewley & Draper’s Dublin-made ink, as will be seen
by their announcement in another part of this paper.
The firm are offering a reward of £10 for such informa-
tion as will lead to the conviction of all or any of the
offenders. We trust that this course of action will
eficctually dig the grave of this false and injurious
rumour.

The Women’s Branch of the Limerick Industrial
Association has initiated a novel and interesting method
of securing the attention of the populace to direct them
ou the path of Irish industrial development—viz., free

_eoncerts and a short lecture as one of the items. These

entertainments it is intended to hold in every parish in
the city during the winter months, and should the suc-
cess of the first held in St. Munchin’s parish be taken
as a standard it is sure to be a most successful and
unique undertaking. The workingmen and their wives
attended to hear the doctrine of practical Irish nationa-
Iity accompanied with the songs and music of their own
country from artistes drawn from their city. - Home
truths were repeated to them in an emphatic manner
hy Mr. Michael O’Callaghan, who wisely said that

ghould one woman leave that hall that night deter-

mined to supplant in her household English or foreign
goods by the native article in future, his words were
not in vain.

Teis. At the Oireachtas, second place was taken in the
competition for the Oireachtas Shield, and one, of .fhe
dramas performed there was written by. a well-known
member, Piaras Beaslai. . The branch has the distinction
of having made the largest branch collection in Dublin
for this year’s Language Fund. Classes, based on the
Dublin Feis programme, are held on Tuesday and Fri-
day nights, from 8 to 9.30 o’clock. A teachers’ ad-

Qanon Peter O’Leary, of Castlelyons, writes in this
issue of the LEADER a very important article, to which
we would specially direct the attention of our readers.
It is doubly important in these days when the country,
judging by all signs, will have to make a stiff fight for
God and for language. All those who have, as parents
or teachers, the responsibility of bringing up children in
the Faith will see the significance of it from the religious
point of view, and those interested in the language
rovival will, apart from its religious aspect, perceive the
importance of Canon O’Leary’s views as a means of
watering the growing Irish language plant.

When purchasing goods as a result of advertisements
in the LEADER, our readers would do this paper a service
by mentioning it as the source of their information.
We have ample reason to know that many already do
50, but in our large circle of readers there must be
many others who omit to mention the name of the
LeADER when doing business with firms as a result of
advertisements in its columns. Now that we have
suggested that references to the LEADER in this connec-
tion would be serviceable to it, we are sure they will
avail of such opportunities in future.

Amongst the articles unavoidably held over this week
is ‘“ Lee’s ”’ reply to his critics.

this, and if he could give me the names of any English
firms or individuals who are paying this price. I can-
not get half the money here in Cork.—Yours faithfully,

TfYHERE were four Yanks in the compartment of

the train that took me from Amiens Street to
Dundalk. ¢ The greenhorns aren’t so green, I guess,”
said one of the ladies—there were two young ladies and
two men ‘‘ guessers.””  Well, I guess they are not.
One of tle men ‘‘ guessers’’ gave three ha’pence and a
sovereign in mistake for four ha’pence to a gossoon whe
carried his bag—a bit green that for a swaggering
Yank. One of the Yanks went into a ‘‘ store’’ for a
whisk, and was naturally offered something with which
to sweep the floor. How funny of the ‘‘ greenhorns.’
It appears that they call something else a whisk in
’Muraca, and we are ‘‘ funny like,”” I suppose, because
we have our own meaning for the word “ whisk !’ The
rattle of the Great Northern carriages commingling with
the twang of the Yanks made a composite noise that
was unintelligible, if unmusical. = Drogheda was the
first stop, and then the human voice had it all to itself
again, ‘‘ Guess there’s no delivery from the grocery
stores here—see women with parcels.”’” And again—
‘“ Yes; there’s a song about the battle of the Boyne,

I guess.”” The ‘‘ guessers’’ changed at Dundalk.
I was on my way to Trillick, in the Co. Tyrone. Our
readers have often heard of ‘‘Irish Dromore.”’” Dro-

more is a parish in Tyrone, in which Father Mathew
Maguire was a curate for some years. Father
Maguire set; himself to Irishise it, and ¢‘ Irish Dromore’
became famous. I have never been to Dromore, so,
beyond a few general remarks, I cannot say anything
of it. In July of last year Father Matt Maguire was
appointed parish priest of the neighbouring parish of
Kilskeery, the capital town of which is Trillick, a
village of about 200 inhabitants.  Kilskeery is an
extensive parish a few miles south of Dromore. The
Irish language has been dead there for a long time. A
few people on the confines of the parish had come in
contact with the Irish movement in Dromore, but with
these exceptions the parish of Kilskeery was as squeezed
of Irish as any parish in Ireland. As soon as Father
Maguire went there, his extraordinary fervour for the
revival of Irish quickly set things stirring. He
was only there a year and a month or two when the
Citt  Sgipe peip startled Ivish Ireland, as well it
might, with its nearly 3,000 entries. I intended
visiting. this parish, this inferesting Trish-Tre-

gathered during the Feis might not be convincing, so
I fulfilled my promise to visit Kilskeery last week. 1

THE AGBICULTURAL RETURNS.

3 HE Agricultural Statistics for 1907 have just been
Z issued by the Department of Agriculture, and
they show a decrease in the area under tillage for the
present year of 40,698 acres, as compared with the total
tillage area of 1906. Of this decrease, 25,139 acres are
assigned to Ulster, the province of small farms and
tillage ; and 11,296 acres to Connaught, the province of
ranches and small tillage area. Leinster shows a net
decrease of 2,464 acres; and Munster a net decrease of
1,799 acres. The one redeeming figure in this dismal
record of declining tillage is that the arca under flax,
in Ulster, shows an increase of 4,466 acres. The com-
piler of the statistics expresses the opinion that the wet
and unfavourable spring weather accounts for the .de-
crease in tillage, especially in the North and the West,
where the land is wet, cold, and exposed. There may
be something in the point, as regards climatic condi-
tions, but one wet spring could not affect the tillage
area, because the land would have been in preparation
for cropping, from the previcus winter, and the seed-
bed would have been made ready, in advance of spring,
and sowing, even though late, should take place—else
- the land would remain idle, as fallow.

As regards meadow and pasture, the total area under
meadow in 1907 is returned at 2,281,263 acres, a de-

grazing provinces—Leinster and Connaught—that the

decrease in the acreage under hay and roots, the pro-

the addition of the latter, which had been previously
classed as waste, would partly account for the increased
area under pasture, but the shrinkage in crops would
approximately account for 89,000 acres. The burden of
these agricultural statistics is, that tillage has decreased
to a considerable extent in Ireland, the present year,
and that the decrease is most noticeable in the North
and the West, where tillage used formerly rule amongst
the smaller farmers. There is no consolidation of farms
now by evieting landlords; the small and medium-sized
holdings are not being converted into large grazing
tracts by them ; and the fact cannot be blinded that the
farmers themselves are turning the land to grass and
relinquishing tillage. What is the cause of this state
of things?

The investigation forms an interesting agricultural
problem. Is the change from tillage to grass due to
taste, does it arise from motives of profits, or has it been .
caused by adverse climatic conditions?  As regards
taste, same might rule with the holder of many acres,
but it certainly would not prevail with a small farmer.

Looking to the returns of the past ten years, this
year’s total of cattle, viz., 4,674,834, is slightly over
the average; but the total of sheep, 3,815,995, is con-:
siderably under the average; so that, although pasture
is oxtending, herds and flocks do not show a correspond-
ing increase, and, therefore, the grass cannot be taken
as sufficiently stocked, and for that reason does not yield
a proper rent. This year’s total of horses, 596,024, is
slightly over the average of the ten years, and the total
of pigs, 1,316,729, is also over it; but taking the all-
round average of live stock—horses, cattle, sheep, and
pigs—the numbers are practically stationary if not
diminished. ~We thus have declining tillage, and ex-
panding pasture, but non-increasing live stock; the

: The most remarkable thing in connection with the
returns is the decline they show in Ulster agriculture,
not only as regards the area under crops, but in a more
marked degree the number of live stock of every
description, with the exception of sheep.

As already mentioned, Ulster shows a decreased area
of 25,139 acres under crops, as compared with last year’s
area, the only exception being the increase of 4,426
acres under flax this year. Again, reverting to the live
stock returns, we find a decrease in Ulster of horses,
mules, and asses, amounting to 2,841; of cattle to
15,005; and of pigs to 12,228; but an increase of
23,683 in sheep. The increase recorded in the latter
may be more apparent than real. There are large
numbers of black-faced sheep fed in Ulster, and their
erumeration must be a difficult matter, on the moun-
tains where they are grazed. Perhaps the increased num-
bers merely represent betler enumeration; but, of
course, if the increase is due to more extended sheep
farming on the lowlands, apart from the mountains,
then the substantial increase recorded would add con-
siderably to the agricultural assets of the province.
However, even making full allowance for the increase of
23,683 sheep, and the money value they represent, same
would not go very far towards reducing the big deficit
arising from the decline in the other classes of live stock,
as well as in crops, disclosed by the agricultural returns
for the province.  There seems to be an obstacle of
some kind retarding agricultural progress in Ulster.

Credit is due the compiler of the statistics for his
clear, concise, and accurate report, which gives a mass
of the most interesting information in handy form suit-
able to all readers. The whole thing is well up-to-date,
and its completeness bespeaks hard work on the part of
the staff concerned, publication having ensued within

For the more flippant section of the audience, thén,
Liere is a recipe. *The flippant people want intellectual
recreation, or think they do—books, the drama, and so
on. Remind them of the fact that they do want these
things, and then proceed with them thus. The hooks
you read, say to them, };hc drama you support, the
music you enjoy, are Knglish. You had no hand in the
making of them, therefore you can take no pride in
them. You had no soul in the development of them,
therefore you ought be suspicious of your extreme liking
for them. You have nothing as good as them of your
own, therefore you ought be ashamed of yourselves.
Pray, be ashamed of yourselves then and learn Irish.
Irish books will be your own, and can’t be anybody
clso’s, cither to boast of or to make money of. = Lrish
drama will have to be acted by Irishmen. Irish music
can be born and bred only in Ircland. For shame,
say to them, you intellectual people, ll}cre you are, in-
dignant at the idea of having to wear English hats, or
lcots, or smoke Hnglish tobacco, but you don’t mind
letting England make your brains for you, and even do
your thinking for you with them. Nay, you are willing
to work hard in order to be able to pay her for obliging
you by doing so. If you made your own brains you
wouldn’t be such fools.

Again, there is the plain, sensible, average man and
woman. They get up in the morning and spend per-
haps twenty minutes at breakfast. At mid-day they
spend another twenty minutes eating, and at night
another. Now, outside that one solitary hour for the
rest of the day they have no enjoyment except what the
mind supplies. In other words, from mental sources
they are deriving enjoyment, or at least have enjoy-
ment to derive, all the day through—for the work of
the mind goes on even during dinner—while of bodily,
animal, enjoyment they have only one hour in the
twenty-four.  And yet, in order to make sure of that
hour’s animal enjoyment, they work the whole day
through, and are sorry they can’t work the night
through too. And not only that, but they have practi-
cally devoted all their young lives to learning how so
to work.  While, on the other hand, they leave
the intellectual enjoyment, the enjoyment of eleven-
twelfths of their hours alive, to look after itself, never
studying what will increase it, never thinking what
might vary it, never even caring to ensure that it will
be there for them at all. As, however, there is well on
to a hundred years of education at the back of this sen-
sible, practical, state of mind in Ireland, there is no
use trying to lift it out of its furrow in five minutes.
All that can be done with it is to tactfully joke with it
about, and get it to laugh at, its abnormal dinner-hour
anxiety. That and gradually to give it such intellec-
tual enjoyment as will suit its appetite. Which enjoy-
ment, of course, can be nothing else but the native
article. That is to say, at annual, and at all pro-
pagandist meetings of the Gaelic League, for the benefit
of the plain, matter of fact, men and women who have
no particalar turn or style of intellect to be appealed to,
the air of the proceedings will have to be depended on,

and should be such that it might be depended on, to do L

the appealing. But while that is so, a few words
about, and a timely laugh at, our sensible people’s
dinner-hour anxiety won’t go down amiss with anybody,
not even the sensible people themselves.

Lastly, at annual meetings there will be the workers;
the few workers, that don’t need to be spoken  to.
There is, however, one lesson of intellectual economy
which even they will thank a chairman for reminding
them of, the lesson, namely, that they themselves never
kuew what intellectual happiness was until they learned
Trish.  Tell them that, for they need to be cheered.
Tell them that, for they need to be encouraged. Tell
them that, for it will inspire them to work with renewed
hope for the day when all Treland will, like ther, have
discovered what intellectual happiness is. And, having
discovered, will bless the memory of the first Gaelic
Teaguers. “Mapdan.”

THE POETRY OF DONNCHADH RUADH.

I T is a hopeful sign that of late not a little discussion
: has centred around the work of our modern Irish
ipoets. - The number of students of their work is plainly
on the increase, and that in itself is very satisfactory.
I6is astonishing how many Gaelic Leaguers one meets—
genuine Leaguers, too—who will confess that they have
‘hardly ever read a line of Irish poetry, or Irish verse
for that matter, and who, though they may road ordi-
aary Irish prose without much difficulty, will be hape-
l_essly at sea when it comes to explaining or understand-
ing a fairly simple Irish poem. Now, however, they
-are, some of them, beginning to he attracted by the
‘wealth of Trish poetry unearthed during these latter
yeaxs by the labours of a devoted few ; they have begun
to nibble at the rich repast they see spread before them,
by-and-by they may hope to be in its full enjoyment.
And if a-few of them have rushed to unfavourable con-
clusions before they have been in a position rightly to
taste of the poctic store, it behoves serious students who
have gone deeper and farther—we are all of us students
in the matter of Irish poetry—to take a good-natured
view of these precocious cbullitions. We can trust to
time to do full and manifest justice to the merits, such
as they are, of our poets.  Most of us, I imagine, will
live to see the day when the verdicts of some of our
present-day critics of Ivish poetry will be regarded in
much the. same light as are now the egregious dicta of
Drs. Atkinson and Mahaffy on Irish literature eight or
nine years ago.

These prefatory remarks have suggested themselves to
me on reading the article by “ S1ohdn *’on Donnchadh
Ruadh in the Leaper. Let me own that T read
her (or his) article with a good deal of pleasure.
There is a decisive ‘‘ whole-hogger >’ flavour about it
which is delightfully refreshing. But, putting aside
for the moment her 1deas as to what constitutes poetry,
I am afraid that, despite her ‘close study’ of
Donnchadh Ruadh (I wonder was it a study for exami-
nation purposes merely ?), she was but very ill equipped
for her self-imposed task of sitting in judgment upon
him.

In the first place ‘ Siobhdn’s ’’ reasoning is based on
the tacit assumption that she has studied all, or at
least the cream, of Donnchadh’s work. That assump-
-tion is quite baseless. O’Flannghaile’s edition does not

- pretend to be complete, or anything like it; the editor

consulted no MSS., contenting himself with merely re-

printing such poems as had been previously published,

viz., the Bachira, and some half-a-dozen shorter pieces.

Several others are known to exist in manuscript, and if

a thorough search were made I doubt not that the

number already published could be trebled. (From 35

to 50 poems each is the usual number Tor Donnchadh’s

contemporaries in editions pretending to any degree of

IRELAND AT WORK.

A CORK CLOTHING FACTORY.

HE cutting of cloth in a clothing factory is a
rather interesting process, as it calls for dex-
terity of hand as well as for machinery. The machine
cuts anything in the nature of clo!;h‘ that comes in its
way, so it is as well to keep at a civil distance from it.
A layer of as many as thirty-five pieces oi cloth were
cut at Lyons and Co.’s factory in' Cork some time back
for suits for a large Irish contract, tl}aL was an un-
usually large number to put to the knife at one time.
The knife is a sort of thin steel bcltmglrunnmg at a
great speed, and working in the perpendicular through
a slot in the table. The operator mf:rc]y guxfles the
layers of cloth in the directions i_n whicl: he wishes to
cut. It is a very simple operation—to look at; bu@,
gentle reader, simple as it looks, one half hour at it
would be sufficient, we feel sure, to enable you to spoil
a great amount of cloth, and cut off at least four of
your fingers. The manufa_c!.ure of suits of‘ clothes
gives scope for a varied division of labour. There are
numbers of ordinary, or more or less ol_'dma.ry, sewing
machines working away ; there are ma({hmes for making-
button-holes, machines for ‘‘quilting ”—our lady
readers will know what quilting is, an_d the men can
ask them if they want further infcrmaticn. TLere are
machines to make up to five rows of quilting’at a time,
and there are braiding machines.

Mr. O’Mullane showed us round the place.  Like
every other man in Ireland who has to send goods_all
over Treland, as his firm does, he Las complaints against
our nation-killing railways. He finds it cheaper to
ship goods to Greenock, and from Greenock to Derry,
than from Cork to Derry direct by rail! One weuld think
that a railway might at least kill Irish trade consis-
tently, but the tariff for certain classes of gcods from
Cork to Athlone, 162} miles, is 25s.; from Cork to
Carlow, 1611 miles, is 34s. 5d.; and Cork to Clare-
morris, 155 miles, is 33s. 1d.; Cork to Kilkenny, 143
miles, 32s. 1d., as against Cork to Bagnalstown via Kil-
kenny, 1573 miles, 25s.! The last case speaks for
itself. We wonder could a Cork trader who
wanted to land goods in Kilkenny book them tc Bag-
nalstown for 25s. a ton instead of 32s. 1d. to Kilkenny,
and take tkem out before their journey was completed?
Mr. O’Mullane gave us several instances, which showed
that two separate bookings totted up to less than one
through booking!  Perhaps simple arithmetic is an
optional subject with some of the responsible railway
officials in Ireland.

A number of men were busily engaged in the press-
ing room pressing out the clothes. The firm have some
of the latest gas pressing machines, but they cannot use
them unless at a prohibitive cost, the Insurance Com-
panies charge being so excessive. It is a strange thing
that in this building gas was first publicly lit in the
city of Cork—that was nearly one hundred years ago,
in 1815, to be precise, and now in 1907 the firm finds
that owing to cause stated they cannot use gas
presser machines.  They make their own -electric
light on the premises. There are, or there
were, at the time of our visit, thirty new mackines
ready to be put up, but .Iabour is wanted. It is re-
markable that whilst emigration is going on various
industries find it difficult to expand for want of labour.
Of course, the explanation is simple enough. Skilled
and efficient labour is partly scarce here, whilst the
market for unskilled labour in many places is over-
stocked, and so there is an overflow to America, though
the prospects there, in competition with the hordes of
emigrants arriving from other countries, caunot be
bright ; that is only one side of tke explanation; when
men go out to America they have to ‘‘look slippy ;’”
here there is too much of the come-day-go-day-God-
send-Sunday mood all over the counfry, and so we
import people that some other country—England, Scot-
land, or Germany—has already trained to ‘look
slippy.””  For the producers in Ireland have to compete
in the open market all the time with their finisked
products.

““ The principal speaker’’ struck me, not with awe,

The proceedings were opened by Dr. Mark Ryan in

in the same strain. One of the gentlemen treated the
audience to the old platitude that unity is indispensable

fused exposition of the ‘“ Sinn Fein Policy *’ in a long
